lessons.title: Fluid balance & IV therapy judgment (NCLEX-RN, Canada)
lessons.seoTitle: Fluid balance & IV therapy for NCLEX-RN | Canada clinical lesson
lessons.seoDescription: Canada RN context: assess volume status, interpret IV orders, and prioritize complications—aligned to NCLEX-RN clinical judgment expectations.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Fluid and electrolyte questions on the NCLEX-RN reward **prioritization**: who needs assessment first, what complication is emerging, and which order is unsafe in context. This lesson uses **Canadian acute care** language (units, common protocols) without mixing US-only scope statements.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Tie **intake and output**, **vitals**, and **labs** together before choosing interventions. For IV therapy, verify **indication**, **rate**, **line type**, and **monitoring**—then connect early signs of fluid overload or deficit to your next action.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: When stem length increases, separate **data** from **noise**: abnormal findings that change risk in the next minutes belong in your first move. Practice stating a one-sentence **clinical concern** before picking an option.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Use **elimination** on dosage or IV rate items when one option ignores assessment, another skips monitoring, or a third assumes stability you have not established. Pair this lesson with a **timed block** in your bank filtered to fluids/electrolytes.

lessons.title: Cardiovascular prioritization for Canadian NCLEX-RN
lessons.seoDescription: Practice stable vs unstable presentations, ischemia red flags, and safe sequencing for Canadian RN candidates preparing for NCLEX-RN.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Cardiovascular items often test whether you recognize **time-sensitive deterioration**—chest discomfort with diaphoresis, sudden hypotension with tachycardia, or new arrhythmia patterns that need escalation now, not later.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Anchor on **perfusion** and **oxygen delivery**: connect blood pressure, heart rate, mentation, and skin findings before choosing teaching, discharge, or medication actions.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: For multi-patient prioritization, choose the client whose **condition is most likely to worsen without immediate nursing action**—not the longest task list.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: After this lesson, run a **question bank** session filtered to cardiovascular topics, then a **mock exam** segment to rehearse stamina.

lessons.title: PN scope & safety foundations (REx-PN, Canada)
lessons.seoTitle: PN scope & safety for REx-PN | Canada practical nursing
lessons.seoDescription: Canada practical nurse pathway: delegation boundaries, scope-aware communication, and safety-first sequencing aligned to REx-PN-style judgment items.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: REx-PN items frequently test **what the PN can do independently**, **what needs clarification**, and **what must be escalated**. Keep **provincial scope** in mind—this lesson uses general PN-safe framing without substituting regulator guidance.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Separate **assessment** from **intervention**: know when your role is to report, observe, or carry out a provider-ordered skill—and when to pause for orders.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: In communication stems, choose responses that **protect the client**, **clarify orders appropriately**, and **avoid practicing outside scope**.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Pair with **PN-tier** question bank blocks and short **practice exams** to build speed without skipping rationales.

lessons.title: Respiratory assessment & oxygenation (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Respiratory assessment for NCLEX-RN | US lesson
lessons.seoDescription: US RN candidates: oxygen delivery, work of breathing, and early escalation patterns for NCLEX-RN clinical judgment practice.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: US NCLEX-RN stems often embed **oxygenation risk** in routine data: subtle SpO₂ changes, accessory muscle use, or altered mental status with lung findings.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Link **airway**, **breathing**, and **circulation** decisions: choose interventions that match the primary threat and the monitoring you will perform next.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: When answers include teaching or discharge, confirm **stability** and **understanding** first—do not select education if assessment is incomplete.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Use **topic filters** in the question bank for respiratory items, then schedule a **timed mock** to consolidate pacing.

lessons.title: Differential reasoning in primary care (FNP track)
lessons.seoTitle: FNP differential reasoning | primary care | NurseNest
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP track: structured differentials, red flags, and follow-up planning—scoped to FNP preparation, not other NP specialties.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: FNP items emphasize **breadth** across ages with **depth** in safe prescribing, screening, and escalation. This lesson is **FNP-specific**—do not substitute PMHNP or AGPCNP hubs for specialty-scoped study.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Build a **problem representation**, then test competing diagnoses with **targeted history**, **exam**, and **initial workup**—avoid premature closure.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: For chronic disease follow-up, align medication changes with **organ function**, **risk**, and **patient capacity**—document reasoning you could defend to a preceptor.

sections.heading: exam tips
sections.body: Cross-link to **NP-tier** bank items tagged for primary care and complete **longer case blocks** to mirror certification pacing.
